macro_segments.title: Introduction to Masechet Zevachim and the Seder Kodashim

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the new tractate and seder
Overview of Zevachim, Menachot, and Chullin within Seder Kodashim
Availability of Talmudic and early rabbinic sources for Kodashim

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Sacrifices Slaughtered Shelo Lishmah (Not for Their Name)

micro_segments.title: Opening mishnah: sacrifices slaughtered not for their proper purpose are kosher but do not fulfill the owner's obligation
Distinction between validity of the sacrifice as an object and satisfaction of the owner's obligation
Exceptions: Pesach and Chatat as sacrifices that become invalid when slaughtered shelo lishmah
Rabbi Eliezer extends the exception to Asham; Yosi ben Choni argues for symmetrical rules across all sacrifices

macro_segments.title: Shimon Achi Azaryah's Ruling on Elevation vs. Degradation of Sacrifices

micro_segments.title: Shimon's principle: slaughtering a sacrifice for a higher level is kosher; for a lower level is invalid
Hierarchy of sacrifices: kodashei kodashim versus kodashim kalim; bechor and maaser relative to shelamim

macro_segments.title: Talmudic Analysis of Shelo Lishmah: The Sanctity Remains Intact

micro_segments.title: The gemara's interpretation of the mishnah's language: שלא לשמן implies active wrong intent, not mere absence of right intent
Rava's ruling: even though the owner's obligation is not fulfilled, the sacrifice retains its full sanctity and cannot be subjected to further improper thoughts
Scriptural basis from Deuteronomy: conditional nature of vow as neder or nedava depending on proper execution

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Inquiry into Lishma Requirements: Zevachim versus Geitin

micro_segments.title: Ravina asks Rav Pappa about Rava's resolution of apparent contradiction between mishna rule and blank-minded slaughter
The distinction between shelo nizbechu lishmah (not slaughtered for purpose) and shnizbechu shelo lishmah (slaughtered for wrong purpose)
Proof that blank intent works for sacrifices but not for gittin; the difference lies in the nature of the objects involved

macro_segments.title: Stam Leshmah: How Sanctified Objects Define Acts

micro_segments.title: Rava's explanation: sacrifices are like designated property; an olah automatically defines acts done to it as olah acts unless actively redefined
Contrast with a woman in divorce: a woman has no inherent designation for divorce, so blank mind does not work for gittin

macro_segments.title: Evidentiary Basis for Blank Intent in Sacrifices

micro_segments.title: Proof from mishnah describing scenarios of lishman and shelo lishman with active different intent, implying blank intent is acceptable
Further proof from mishnah listing six considerations; Rabbi Yosi says even if none are in mind, the sacrifice is kosher because of beis din's condition
Beis din's ruling: Kohanim are instructed not to think so as to avoid wrong thoughts, proving blank intent is valid

macro_segments.title: Evidentiary Basis for Requiring Lishma in Gittin

micro_segments.title: Marketplace example: a sample get written generically cannot be used by someone whose name matches, even by coincidence
Distinguishing cases where get was written with intent to divorce a specific woman but names were generic
Case of two wives with same names: get written for one wife cannot be used for the other, showing need for specific lishma
Blank form gittin: must leave blanks for man's name, woman's name, and the permissive formula to avoid invalidity

macro_segments.title: The Conceptual Difference: Objects with Inherent Identity versus Objects Requiring Definition

micro_segments.title: Summary: sanctified sacrifices have inherent identity that defines the nature of acts performed upon them; blank intent therefore does not invalidate
Summary: gittin lack inherent designation to any woman; the document must be created with specific woman in mind, requiring explicit lishma

topical_tags.term: Stam leshmah omed (blank intent counts as proper)
Tenai Beis Din (condition stipulated by Beis Din)
